title: NOC 83121 – Fishermen/women | GTR Immigration
description: Fishermen/women under NOC 83121 are the essential skilled workers operating Canada’s commercial fishing vessels to harvest fish and other marine life. As a TEER 3 occupation, these professionals are distinct from entry-level deckhands; they possess the technical expertise to operate specialized fishing gear, maintain vessel systems, and manage catch preservation. In 2026, as Canada’s "Blue Economy" expands with a focus on sustainable, high-value exports, these skilled harvesters are in high demand across the Atlantic and Pacific coasts. For immigration, this role is a primary candidate for the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the New 2026 Sector-Specific Stream for Agriculture and Fish Processing.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level3Major Group83 – Occupations in natural resources and related productionSub-Major Group831 – Occupations in natural resources and related productionMinor Group8312 – Fishing vessel masters and fishermen/womenNOC Code83121

duties: Operate fishing gear (e.g., seines, trawls, gillnets, traps) to catch fish and other marine life.
Clean, sort, and pack fish or shellfish in ice and stow them in the vessel’s hold.
Repair and maintain fishing gear, including nets, lines, and traps.
Operate and maintain deck machinery such as winches, cranes, and hydraulic haulers.
Assist in navigating the vessel and perform lookout duties under the direction of the Master.
Ensure the catch is handled according to strict food safety and quality standards for export.
Participate in emergency drills and ensure all safety equipment is operational.
employment_requirements: Education: Some secondary school education is usually required.
Experience: Several years of experience as a Fishing Vessel Deckhand (NOC 85102) are mandatory to move into this skilled category.

Certifications:Marine Emergency Duties (MED) certificate is mandatory.A Radio Operator’s Certificate (ROC-MC) is often required.A valid Fisherman's Registration Card issued by the Department of Fisheries and Oceans (DFO).
Marine Emergency Duties (MED) certificate is mandatory.
A Radio Operator’s Certificate (ROC-MC) is often required.
A valid Fisherman's Registration Card issued by the Department of Fisheries and Oceans (DFO).
Skills: Strong physical stamina, the ability to work in varying weather conditions at sea, and mechanical aptitude are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: Because many harvesters are paid a share of the catch, annual income can fluctuate. Successful crews in high-value fisheries (like Snow Crab or Lobster) often exceed the "High" estimates.
job_outlook: The outlook is Very Good. According to the Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS), the commercial fishing sector will face a labor shortage of over 4,500 workers by 2033 due to retirements. As Canada invests in "Value-Added" seafood processing, the demand for skilled harvesters who can provide high-quality raw materials will remain a top priority.
labour_market_demand: The demand for skilled fishermen/women is Favourable, particularly in the Maritime provinces and British Columbia. In 2026, Nova Scotia, Newfoundland and Labrador, and Prince Edward Island are facing a significant "demographic cliff" as veteran harvesters retire. This has opened a massive window for international workers with maritime experience to join commercial fleets specializing in lobster, crab, and groundfish.
High Earning Potential: Many Canadian commercial fishing roles operate on a "catch-share" basis, allowing skilled workers to earn significantly high incomes during peak seasons.
Modern Fleet Technology: Work with advanced sonar, GPS-integrated gear, and automated hauling systems.
World-Class Fisheries Management: Join a sector recognized globally for its science-based sustainability and conservation efforts.
Direct Path to PR: Recognized as an "Essential Trade" in coastal provinces with dedicated immigration pathways for maritime workers.
pathways: New 2026 Sector-Specific Stream: IRCC has prioritized Agriculture and Fish Processing as a high-demand sector for targeted PR invitations.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): The fastest route for maritime workers with a job offer in Atlantic Canada.
Express Entry (Category-Based Selection): Targeted under the "Trade" or "Agriculture/Natural Resources" categories.
BC PNP Skills Immigration: A dedicated pathway for those working in B.C.'s commercial fishing and aquaculture sectors.
